text: Consumer law in India protects every buyer of goods and services from unfair trade practices, defective products, deficient services, and exploitation by sellers and manufacturers. The main law is the Consumer Protection Act 2019 which replaced the old 1986 Act. Every person who buys goods or hires services for personal use is a consumer with legal rights. This includes purchases from shops, online platforms, hospitals, banks, insurance companies, builders, telecom companies, and government utilities. In Uttar Pradesh, consumer disputes are resolved through a three-tier system: District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (DCDRC) at district level, UP State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission in Lucknow, and National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC) in New Delhi. Filing a consumer complaint is simple, inexpensive, and does not require a lawyer. The Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) set up under the 2019 Act can take suo motu action against companies for widespread unfair practices. UP has District Consumer Forums in all 75 districts.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019

text: Who is a consumer under the Consumer Protection Act 2019? A consumer is any person who buys goods or hires services for personal use and not for resale or commercial purpose. Online buyers are also consumers. A person who buys goods as a gift is also a consumer. If you buy goods for running a business you are NOT a consumer under this Act. Beneficiaries of services are covered even if they did not directly pay — for example a patient treated under someone else's insurance. NRIs who buy goods or services in India are covered. Who is NOT a consumer: a person who buys goods for resale; a person in an employer-employee relationship getting services; persons availing completely free services. The 2019 Act widened the definition to include all electronic and online transactions. In UP, any resident who has purchased goods or availed services and faced deficiency can approach the District Consumer Forum of their district without needing to hire a lawyer.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(7)

text: What are the six fundamental rights of every consumer in India? Right 1 — Right to Safety: protection against goods and services hazardous to life and property. Right 2 — Right to Information: the right to know the quality, quantity, potency, purity, standard, and price of goods and services before buying. Right 3 — Right to Choose: access to variety of goods and services at competitive prices. Right 4 — Right to be Heard: assurance that consumer interests receive proper attention at appropriate forums. Right 5 — Right to Seek Redressal: right to seek settlement of genuine grievances against unfair trade practices or exploitation. Right 6 — Right to Consumer Education: the right to acquire knowledge and skills to be an informed consumer. These six rights are enshrined in the Consumer Protection Act 2019 and form the foundation of all consumer protection in India. In UP, the Consumer Awareness programmes under Jaago Grahak Jaago campaign are run by the Department of Consumer Affairs to educate citizens about these rights.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2

text: What kind of complaints can be filed under Consumer Protection Act 2019? Defective goods: goods that are faulty, substandard, or do not conform to promised quality. Deficient services: services falling short of the standard promised — poor hospital treatment, bad contractor work, bank errors. Unfair trade practices: misleading advertisements, false claims, selling above MRP, refusing to issue bills, bait and switch tactics. Overcharging: charging more than MRP or agreed price. Selling hazardous goods without proper warnings. Unfair contracts: one-sided contract clauses that disadvantage consumers. Online fraud and e-commerce cheating: fake products, non-delivery, refund denial. Medical negligence, builder delays, insurance claim rejection, and telecom service failure are also covered. In UP, common complaints include: shops charging above MRP; private hospitals overcharging; builders delaying flat possession in Noida, Greater Noida, Lucknow; insurance companies rejecting claims; banks making unauthorized deductions. All these can be filed at the District Consumer Forum of the relevant district in UP.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(6), 2(11), 2(47)

text: Which consumer forum should you approach and what are the monetary limits in UP? District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (District Forum): handles complaints where value of goods or services and compensation claimed is up to Rs 50 lakh. In UP, all 75 districts have a District Consumer Forum. State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (State Commission): handles complaints where value exceeds Rs 50 lakh but is up to Rs 2 crore. UP State Consumer Commission is located in Lucknow — address: State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission UP, Vibhuti Khand, Gomti Nagar, Lucknow. National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC): handles complaints where value exceeds Rs 2 crore, located in New Delhi. Appeals: District Forum order can be appealed to State Commission within 45 days; State Commission to NCDRC within 30 days; NCDRC to Supreme Court within 30 days. Filing fees are nominal and increase with claim value. For UP consumers, the District Forum in their own district is the most accessible starting point.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 34, 47, 58

text: How to file a consumer complaint in Uttar Pradesh? Step 1: Send a legal notice to the seller or service provider by registered post demanding remedy within 15 to 30 days. Keep a copy. Step 2: If no satisfactory response, file complaint at the District Consumer Forum of your district or online at edaakhil.nic.in. Step 3: The complaint must include your name and address, name and address of opposite party, facts of the case in chronological order, supporting documents such as bill, warranty card, photographs, and correspondence, and relief claimed such as replacement, refund, or compensation. Step 4: Pay filing fee — up to Rs 5 lakh claim fee is Rs 200; Rs 5 lakh to Rs 10 lakh fee is Rs 400; Rs 10 lakh to Rs 20 lakh fee is Rs 500; Rs 20 lakh to Rs 50 lakh fee is Rs 2,000. Step 5: File in person at the District Consumer Forum office or online at edaakhil.nic.in. A lawyer is not required. In UP, the e-Daakhil portal has made filing complaints from home possible for all UP residents without visiting the forum office.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019 Section 35, e-Daakhil Portal edaakhil.nic.in

text: What are the time limits for consumer complaints in UP? The consumer complaint must be filed within 2 years from the date the cause of action arose — meaning from the date of defect, deficiency, or unfair practice. If filed after 2 years, the consumer must explain the delay and the forum may condone it if satisfied with the reason. Once a complaint is filed, the District Forum must decide it within 3 months if no lab testing is required and within 5 months if lab testing is needed. The 2019 Act introduced strict timelines to prevent delays which were rampant under the old 1986 Act. Appeal against District Forum order must be filed within 45 days. Appeal against State Commission order must be filed within 30 days. The new 2019 Act also introduced mediation — the forum may refer cases to mediation at Consumer Mediation Cells attached to each Commission for faster resolution. UP State Commission in Lucknow has an active mediation cell. Mediation settlements are passed as orders of the Consumer Commission and are final and binding.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 35(1), 58, 69, Consumer Protection (Mediation) Regulations 2020

text: What are consumer rights in online shopping and e-commerce in India? The Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020 specifically protect online shoppers. Every e-commerce platform like Amazon, Flipkart, Meesho, Myntra must: display country of origin of all goods; display seller name and contact information clearly; not manipulate prices unfairly; have a clear return and refund policy displayed prominently; not cancel orders without consumer consent; acknowledge complaints within 48 hours and resolve within 1 month. Fake reviews, misleading product descriptions, and hidden charges are prohibited. If an online seller delivers a defective or wrong product, the consumer can demand full refund or replacement. If the platform refuses, file a complaint against both the seller and the platform at the District Consumer Forum or at consumerhelpline.gov.in. The National Consumer Helpline 1800-11-4000 has tie-ups with major e-commerce companies for faster resolution. UP consumers buying from online platforms can file their complaint at the District Consumer Forum of their own district.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020, Consumer Protection Act 2019

text: What are consumer rights in case of medical negligence in UP? Medical services are covered under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. Patients and families are consumers; hospitals, doctors, nursing homes, and diagnostic labs are service providers. If a doctor or hospital is negligent resulting in wrong treatment, wrong diagnosis, surgical errors, or death, a consumer complaint can be filed at the District Consumer Forum. The complaint can seek compensation for cost of treatment, loss of income, mental agony, and in case of death, compensation to dependants. Expert medical opinion is often required to prove negligence. Important UP-specific points: complaints against government hospitals can also be filed with the UP Health Department or Chief Medical Officer of the district; private hospital overcharging complaints can be filed with the UP Private Hospital Regulatory Authority; any hospital denying emergency treatment to accident or assault victims can be reported to the CMO. Free services at government hospitals are not covered under Consumer Protection Act — patients using paid services at government hospitals are covered. Consumer complaints against Lucknow, Kanpur, Varanasi, Agra government medical colleges can be filed at the respective District Consumer Forum.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Indian Medical Council Regulations, UP Health Department

text: What are consumer rights regarding insurance claim rejection in UP? Insurance services are fully covered under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. If an insurance company wrongfully rejects a claim, delays settlement, pays less than entitled, or misrepresents policy terms, the policyholder can take action. Escalation steps: first raise complaint with the insurance company grievance redressal cell; if unresolved within 30 days, approach the Insurance Ombudsman. For UP residents, the Insurance Ombudsman for Lucknow region covers all UP districts — office at Jeevan Bhawan Phase II, 6th Floor, Nawal Kishore Road, Hazratganj, Lucknow; email bimaou.lucknow@gmail.com; phone 0522-2231331. If still unresolved or claim exceeds Ombudsman limits, file at District Consumer Forum. IRDAI Bima Bharosa portal at bimabharosa.irdai.gov.in also accepts complaints. IRDAI Bima Bharosa helpline: 155255. Motor accident insurance disputes can go to both MACT and Consumer Forum. Health insurance claim rejections are among the most common consumer complaints in UP — always read policy exclusions carefully and keep all hospital bills and discharge summaries.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Insurance Ombudsman Rules 2017, IRDAI Bima Bharosa

text: What can homebuyers do if a builder delays possession or cheats in UP? Builder and real estate complaints in UP are covered under two laws: Consumer Protection Act 2019 and RERA (Real Estate Regulatory Authority) Act 2016. Under UP-RERA: if a builder delays possession beyond the agreed date, the buyer is entitled to interest at SBI MCLR plus 2% for every month of delay; if builder fails to deliver, buyer can claim full refund with interest. File RERA complaint at UP-RERA Lucknow — website up-rera.in; helpline 0522-2238494; office at RERA Bhawan, Vibhuti Khand, Gomti Nagar, Lucknow. Under Consumer Protection Act: file at District Consumer Forum for deficient service including poor construction quality, false advertisements about amenities, and delayed possession. Both RERA and Consumer Forum complaints can be filed simultaneously. UP has one of the highest volumes of builder complaints in India especially in Noida, Greater Noida, Lucknow, Ghaziabad, and Agra. The Allahabad High Court has passed several judgments directing UP-RERA to expedite builder complaint resolution.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, RERA Act 2016, UP-RERA, up-rera.in

text: What are consumer rights against banks and NBFCs in UP? Banking and financial services are fully covered under Consumer Protection Act 2019. Common complaints include: unauthorized debit or transaction, ATM cash not dispensed but account debited, loan disbursement issues, excessive charges, wrongful cheque bounce, credit card fraud, account freezing without notice, and poor locker service. Escalation steps: first file complaint with the bank branch manager; then escalate to the bank's nodal officer; if unresolved within 30 days, approach the RBI Integrated Ombudsman at bankingombudsman.rbi.org.in or call 14448 toll free — covers all commercial banks, cooperative banks, and NBFCs. For UP, the RBI Regional Office in Lucknow also handles escalated complaints. For compensation claims, file consumer complaint at District Consumer Forum. Digital payment fraud must be reported immediately to the bank and at cybercrime.gov.in or call 1930. RBI has mandated that banks must resolve complaints within 30 days — if they fail, they must pay compensation of Rs 100 per day of delay beyond 30 days. Keep all transaction references and correspondence as evidence.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme 2021

text: What are consumer rights against telecom companies in UP? Telecom companies like Jio, Airtel, BSNL, and Vi are service providers under Consumer Protection Act 2019. Common complaints: excess billing, unauthorized balance deductions, poor network or internet quality, wrongful SIM disconnection, number portability refusal, and SIM swap fraud. Escalation steps: Step 1 — raise complaint with company customer care and get a complaint reference number; Step 2 — if unresolved within 30 days, escalate to the telecom company's Appellate Authority; Step 3 — if still unresolved, file complaint with TRAI at pgportal.gov.in or call TRAI helpline 1800-110-420; Step 4 — for compensation, file consumer complaint at District Consumer Forum. BSNL complaints in UP can also be raised at the BSNL UP Circle office in Lucknow. For mobile number misuse or fraudulent calls, report at Sanchar Saathi portal sancharsaathi.gov.in. For disconnection of mobile number wrongfully, TRAI regulations require restoration within 24 hours of resolution. UP has large BSNL rural coverage — BSNL service complaints from rural UP are common and should be escalated to TRAI if unresolved.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, TRAI Telecom Consumer Protection Regulations 2012

text: What are consumer rights regarding food adulteration and substandard food in UP? The Food Safety and Standards Act 2006 (FSSAI Act) governs food safety across India. Selling adulterated, misbranded, or substandard food is a serious offence with penalties up to life imprisonment for adulteration causing death. How to report in UP: file complaint with the Food Safety Officer (FSO) of your district under the UP Food Safety and Drug Administration (FSDA); file complaint on FSSAI portal at foscos.fssai.gov.in; file consumer complaint at District Consumer Forum for compensation. UP FSDA office is in Lucknow — helpline 1800-180-5533. Common adulteration issues in UP: milk adulteration with urea or detergent; synthetic sweets especially during festival seasons; adulterated mustard oil and ghee; substandard spices and pulses. The UP government conducts regular food sampling drives especially before Holi, Diwali, and Eid. Consumers can demand the FBO (Food Business Operator) license of any food establishment before buying. Restaurants and dhabas must display FSSAI license number. Buying food from unlicensed vendors carries risk — complaints about such vendors go to the local FSO.

metadata.source: Food Safety and Standards Act 2006, FSSAI, UP Food Safety and Drug Administration

text: What is product liability under the Consumer Protection Act 2019? The Consumer Protection Act 2019 for the first time expressly recognizes product liability in India. A manufacturer, seller, or service provider can be held liable for harm caused by a defective product or deficient service without the consumer needing to prove negligence. Grounds for product liability claim: manufacturing defect; failure to conform to express warranty; inadequate instructions for use; unreasonably dangerous product design. Compensation can be claimed for personal injury, property damage, and death caused by the defective product. This applies to all products sold in India including imported goods. Common UP product liability cases include: spurious medicines sold at pharmacies; defective electrical appliances causing fire or injury; contaminated packaged food; defective LPG cylinders causing accidents. For spurious medicines, complaint also goes to UP Drug Controller at UP FSDA. For defective LPG cylinders, complaint goes to the oil company distributor and simultaneously to the District Consumer Forum. Product liability claims are filed at the District Consumer Forum for claims up to Rs 50 lakh.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Chapter VI, Section 82-87

text: What are the rules on MRP, weights, and measures and how to complain in UP? MRP (Maximum Retail Price) is the highest price at which a product can be sold to the end consumer — no shopkeeper or seller can charge above MRP. This is governed by the Legal Metrology Act 2009 and the Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules 2011. Rules every consumer should know: every packaged commodity must display MRP, net quantity, date of manufacture, expiry date, and manufacturer details; selling above MRP is a criminal offence; weighing scales must be government-stamped and calibrated; selling less than stated quantity is an offence. How to complain in UP: file complaint with the Legal Metrology Officer of the district (under UP Weights and Measures Department); file consumer complaint at District Consumer Forum; for MRP violations at large retail chains, also report to the Central Consumer Protection Authority at consumerhelpline.gov.in. Common UP violations: hotels and restaurants charging above MRP on bottled water and beverages — this is illegal; cinemas charging above MRP on water and food is also illegal as clarified by NCDRC. Hotels in Lucknow, Agra, Varanasi are frequently penalized for MRP violations.

metadata.source: Legal Metrology Act 2009, Consumer Protection Act 2019, CCPA Guidelines

text: What are unfair trade practices and how are consumers protected? Under Consumer Protection Act 2019, unfair trade practices include: false representation that goods are of a particular standard, quality, or grade when they are not; false claim about sponsorship, approval, or affiliation; false representation about price savings or discounts; conducting prize schemes or contests with intent not to give the promised prize; withholding information about defects in goods; issuing false warranties or guarantees. Misleading advertisements are specifically addressed — the CCPA (Central Consumer Protection Authority) can: order withdrawal of misleading advertisements; ban the advertiser from advertising for up to 1 year; impose penalty up to Rs 10 lakh on manufacturer and up to Rs 10 lakh on celebrity endorser. Common unfair practices in UP: fake gold schemes and chit funds promising unrealistic returns; real estate advertisements showing amenities never delivered; tour operators selling packages with hidden charges; coaching institutes in Prayagraj and Lucknow making false placement claims. Complaints about unfair trade practices can be filed at consumerhelpline.gov.in, CCPA, or the District Consumer Forum.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(47), CCPA Guidelines on Misleading Advertisements 2022

text: What is the National Consumer Helpline and how to use it? The National Consumer Helpline (NCH) is run by the Government of India to help consumers resolve complaints before approaching Consumer Forums. Helpline: 1800-11-4000 or 14404 — toll free, Monday to Saturday 8 AM to 8 PM. Online portal: consumerhelpline.gov.in — file complaints for all types of consumer grievances. The portal has tie-ups with over 1000 companies including major e-commerce, telecom, banking, insurance, and FMCG companies for faster resolution. In UP, the State Consumer Helpline also operates. The Jaago Grahak Jaago mobile app also allows complaint filing. If the company resolves the complaint through the helpline, there is no need to go to the Consumer Forum. For complaints not resolved through NCH, the consumer can escalate to the District Consumer Forum. UP District Consumer Forum addresses and contact details are available at consumerhelpline.gov.in. CCPA also accepts complaints about systemic unfair practices affecting large numbers of consumers. All services are free of cost for consumers.

metadata.source: National Consumer Helpline, Department of Consumer Affairs, Government of India

text: What is the e-Daakhil portal and how does it work for UP consumers? e-Daakhil (edaakhil.nic.in) is the official online portal for filing consumer complaints in India, launched by the Government of India in 2020. It allows consumers to file complaints, pay fees, upload documents, track case status, and attend hearings online without physically visiting the Consumer Forum. How to file on e-Daakhil: register at edaakhil.nic.in with mobile number and email; select the state (Uttar Pradesh) and the District Consumer Forum of your district; fill complaint details including opposite party information, facts, and relief sought; upload supporting documents such as bills, warranty cards, and correspondence; pay filing fee online via UPI, debit card, or net banking; on submission, a unique complaint number is issued for tracking. Hearings can be attended via video conferencing. Case status can be tracked online using the complaint number. This portal is especially useful for UP consumers in smaller districts like Sonbhadra, Chitrakoot, Shravasti, and Balrampur who would otherwise have to travel long distances to file complaints. e-Daakhil is available 24x7. All 75 District Consumer Forums in UP are connected to the e-Daakhil system.

metadata.source: e-Daakhil Portal edaakhil.nic.in, Consumer Protection Act 2019

text: Common real life consumer law situations in UP and where to go. Bought defective mobile from Lucknow or Kanpur shop — send legal notice, then file at District Consumer Forum for replacement or refund. Online order not delivered and refund denied — file at consumerhelpline.gov.in or District Consumer Forum against both seller and platform. Private hospital overcharged or negligent treatment in UP — file at District Consumer Forum for refund and compensation. Insurance claim wrongfully rejected — approach Insurance Ombudsman Lucknow first at 0522-2231331, then District Consumer Forum. Builder delayed flat possession in Noida, Greater Noida, or Lucknow — file with UP-RERA at up-rera.in and simultaneously at District Consumer Forum. Bank debited money wrongfully — report to bank, if unresolved file at RBI Ombudsman bankingombudsman.rbi.org.in or call 14448. Expired food product sold at supermarket — report to UP FSDA on 1800-180-5533 and file consumer complaint. Telecom company deducting balance illegally — escalate to TRAI at pgportal.gov.in. Shop charging above MRP — report to Legal Metrology Officer and file at District Consumer Forum. All consumer complaints can also be filed online at edaakhil.nic.in from home.

metadata.source: Consumer Protection Act 2019, UP-RERA, UP FSDA, Insurance Ombudsman Lucknow, RBI Ombudsman
